Anja Scheurer erläutert: "Grundsätzlich arbeiten wir eng auf Programmebene, sowohl aber auch eben auf der Projektebene mit den jeweiligen Verantwortlichen zusammen." Das Change Management-Team fungiert als Cross-Workstream im Programm und gibt die Methodik und den Change- und User-Kommunikationsplan auf Programmebene vor.

Die Verbindung von Change- und Projekt Management 

Change Management und Projektmanagement waren bei der SAP Cloud Migration von Siemens Healthineers eng verzahnt:

  • Integration: Change Management als Cross-Workstream im Gesamtprogramm.
  • Klare Rollen: Deutliche Aufgabenteilung zwischen PMO und Change Team.
  • Operative Einbindung: Change-Experten in Projektterminen und Cutover-Meetings.
  • Kommunikation: Während das PMO die interne Programmkommunikation vorantrieb, fokussierte sich das Change Management Team auf die Nutzer- und Nutzenkommunikation.
  • Ressourcenbereitstellung: Das Change Management Team stellte den Projektleitern Werkzeuge und Materialien zur Verfügung, um Change-Aktivitäten umzusetzen.

Diese enge Zusammenarbeit ermöglichte eine durchgängige Berücksichtigung von Change-Aspekten und schnelle Reaktionen auf Herausforderungen im Projektverlauf. 

Bewährte Methoden und Tools im Change Management

Bei den eingesetzten Methoden und Tools hat sich das Team anfangs am ADKAR-Modell orientiert, dieses aber an die spezifischen Bedürfnisse des Projekts angepasst. Bewährt haben sich klassische Stakeholder-Analysen, Engagement-Pläne und Change-Readiness-Checks. Blaesing betont auch die Wichtigkeit eines guten People-Networks und vielfältiger Kommunikationskanäle.

Learnings und Tipps für erfolgreiche SAP Migrationsprojekte

Als wichtigste Lernerfahrung nennt Philipp Blaesing die frühzeitige Einbindung des Change Managements:

".. auf jeden Fall das Change Management so früh wie möglich zu involvieren, also schon in den Planungsphasen." Anja Scheurer ergänzt: "Unterschätzt den Bedarf einfach überhaupt nicht für das Change Management und der Kommunikation in Migrationsprojekten, bindet eben die Kollegen und Kolleginnen früh ein."

Auch gaben beide konkrete Tipps für die Praxis:

  • Eine klare RACI-Matrix für Change- und Kommunikations-verantwortlichkeiten erstellen
  • Vielfältige Kommunikationskanäle nutzen (Newsletter, Webseiten, Q&A-Sessions ... )
  • Regelmäßige Feedback-Runden mit Stakeholdern durchführen
  • Change Management als offiziellen Cross-Workstream im Programm etablieren

Inspirierende Aussagen aus dem Podcast

"Wenn man das Gefühl hat, man kommuniziert zu viel, dann war es richtig."
"Change Management ist sehr viel mehr in den Mittelpunkt gerückt als vor fünf bis zehn Jahren und gewinnt immer mehr an Wichtigkeit."

Resümee

Die Erfahrungen von Siemens Healthineers zeigen, dass eine frühzeitige Einbindung, klare Strukturen und vielfältige Kommunikation entscheidend sind. 

Was ich wichtig finde ist auch folgendes: Change Management erweist sich auch bei primär technischen Projekten als wichtiger Erfolgsfaktor. Die Handlungsfelder sind vielleicht etwas anders. So gibt es beispielsweise wenig End-User Training. Aber Änderungen die Prozesse, Jobs und Aufgaben betreffen gibt es auch bei technischen Projekten. Und auch Fragen zum Mehrwert eines solchen technischen Projekts und die Besprechung und Kommunikation dieser Vorteile. Und hier ist eben Change Management sehr wichtig mit all seinen Facetten, von Stakeholder Analysen bis Kommunikation

⁉️ Habt ihr weitere Beispiele, was bei sehr technischen Projekten besonders ist rund um Change Management? Freue mich auf Kommentare hier auch. 

Shownotes

Buch: Die 5 Sprachen der Mitarbeitermotivation

Buch: Der Change Code von Dieter Lederer

Doku der Community-Session mit Anja zum SAP Change Starterkit

SAP News: Siemens Healthineers entscheidet sich für RISE with SAP um Leistung zu steigern

Aus-und Weiterbildung bei Siemens Healthineers

entry.title: Routing: Alternative Sequence & Parallel Sequence in SAP
entry.content:
When creating routing in SAP, there are 3 different types of sequences that you can create:

    • Standard Sequence

      A standard sequence is the sequence of operations that describes a production process and from which parallel and alternative sequences can branch. 

      When we create a routing, the system will automatically create a sequence, the standard sequence. The other sequence is either an alternative or a parallel sequence, but they will be connected with the standard routing. Alternative sequences are only for routings, but parallel sequences can be used for routings and rate routings.

    • Alternative Sequence

      An alternative sequence is a sequence of operations that can be used as an alternative to a number of consecutive operations from the standard sequence. This enables you to produce a product in different ways.

      Alternative sequences are used, for example, if the production process differs according to lot size, or if there is a downtime. This is also useful when there is capacity overload, and resource or machine breakdown.

      In creating a new routing sequence, you can go to routing CA01 and choose the Sequences icon.



      Choose the new entry icon to select which sequence you want to create.

      In the sequence details, decide the reference data by entering the branch operation and return operation. 

      Branch operation is from which an alternative sequence is to replace the reference sequence. This is the point in the routing where the system leaves the main sequence and enters an alternative sequence. It serves as the decision point to switch from the standard sequence to an alternative sequence.

       

      Return operation is the operation where the system exits the alternative sequence and returns to the main sequence. After the alternative sequence is completed, production resumes from the return operation, ensuring continuity in the production process.

      Meanwhile the alternative sequence fields are filled with the range of lot size of the production. For Example, the lot sizes are from 200 to 500. So, the system will automatically trigger to use the alternative sequence if the production order lot size is 200 or more until 500.

      • Parallel Sequence

        A parallel sequence enables you to process several operations at the same time and is carried out as the corresponding section in the standard sequence. These are special types of overlapping operations or if several operations are processed simultaneously.  This is the case in the process industry.

        This is useful for minimizing lead times, increasing output, and improving utilization. For example, when we handle complex products, when there is high volume production, also important if there’s no dependency between 2 operations.

        But there are considerations of using parallel sequences which will be complex in planning, monitoring, and resource availability.

        To create a parallel sequence, choose the parallel sequence when creating a new entry.
        In the sequence details, decide the reference data by entering the branch operation and return operation. For example:

        Branch operation is set for operation 0060 which is visual inspection. Then, the Return operation is set for operation 0070 which is labelling. This means all operations from 0060 to 0070 are running at the same time or processed simultaneously.

        So that’s basically the way you create a new routing sequence. This sequence will impact on the production order creation after the MRP runs are created. The difference of the sequence will determine which workstation or resource will be used in the production execution.

        Cheers,
        Dafina

entry.title: EWM Layout-Oriented Storage Control (LOSC) – A simple guide
entry.content:
This post will show you how to set up and test Layout-Oriented Storage Control (LOSC) in SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM). We'll focus on the customization and steps to follow the LOSC process. To start, let's imagine this scenario in an imaginary warehouse:



Step 1: Arrival at the Warehouse (Storage Type 9010)
Goods arrive at the warehouse dock and are unloaded into storage type 9010. They are scanned and entered the EWM system, starting their journey.

Step 2: Quality Inspection (Storage Type 0030)
Next, the goods move to storage type 0030 for quality inspection. Here, each item is checked to ensure it meets the required standards.

Step 3: Intermediate Storage (Storage Type 0035)
After passing the quality check, the goods are transferred to storage type 0035, an intermediate storage area. This is a temporary holding zone.

Step 4: Final Storage (Storage Type 0040)
Finally, the goods reach storage type 0040, the final storage area. Here, products are organized and stored until they are ready to be picked up and shipped to customers.


CUSTOMIZING STEPS

  • SCM Extended Warehouse Management -> Extended Warehouse Management -> Master Data -> Define Storage Types

Note: ensure that you have created the activity areas and storage bins for the storage types used in this process. Also, execute the bins sorting.

 

 

 

  • SCM Extended Warehouse Management -> Extended Warehouse Management -> Cross-Process Settings -> Warehouse Task -> Define Warehouse Process Type

Note: The WTP for "LOSC" was copied from 1010. Ensure the "Select HU w/o Storage Process" field is checked, as LOSC only works for handling unit warehouse tasks. If unchecked, a product warehouse task will be created, and the process won't work as intended.

 

  • SCM Extended Warehouse Management -> Extended Warehouse Management -> Cross-Process Settings -> Warehouse Task -> Define Control Indicators for Determining Warehouse Process Types

 

  • SCM Extended Warehouse Management -> Extended Warehouse Management -> Cross-Process Settings -> Warehouse Task -> Determine Warehouse Process Type

 

  • SCM Extended Warehouse Management -> Extended Warehouse Management -> Cross-Process Settings -> Warehouse Task -> Define Layout-Oriented Storage Process Control

 Note: In this scenario, we're using the same WPT for LOSC movements. For better traceability, you can create a WPT copied from 3030 to separate the LOSC steps.


TESTING
 

  • Step 1 – Goods receipt

    T-Code: MIGO

Inboud delivery created:

 

  • Step 2 – Inbound Delivery

T-Code: /SCWM/PRDI
Note: WT we’re created automatically using a PPF action but this is a subject to another post.


Note: HU we’re automatically created using packing specification.

 

  • Step 3 – Warehouse Management Monitor

T-Code: /SCWM/MON
Two warehouse tasks (WT) were created because we are using LOSC. The first warehouse task remains with status "B – Waiting":


The second warehouse task uses WPT "3030" to move the handling unit from 9010 to 0030 and has the status "Open":


Confirm the second WT:

A third warehouse task is created automatically when the second is confirmed:


The first WT has the source storage type and bin updated but remains with status "B – Waiting":


The third warehouse task is created to move the HU from 0030 to 0035:

Confirm the third WT:


The first WT has been updated and then the last move of HU is done:


Confirm the first WT:

Process done!


CONCLUSION

Using SAP EWM LOSC helps the warehouse efficiently manage the flow of goods through different storage types. This process ensures products are inspected, stored, and organized in the best way possible, leading to faster order fulfillment and higher customer satisfaction.

I try to make it simple, hope this guide helps you guys.

Best Regards,
Yuri Nogueira
SAP EWM Consultant

entry.title: Implico at the SAP for Energy and Utilities Conference 2025
entry.content:
As the energy sector navigates rapid digital transformation and sustainability challenges, Implico is at the forefront of delivering innovative solutions to optimize supply chains and enhance fuel retail operations. From April 8-10, 2025, in Rotterdam, we will join leading energy and technology experts at the SAP for Energy and Utilities Conference and showcase how cutting-edge automation and SAP solutions can empower companies to adapt and thrive in a changing market. 

We are proud to present two key sessions at this event: a workshop session on the Pre-Conference Day April 8 and a co-customer presentation at the main event on April 9, both featuring real-world applications of our solutions.

 

1. Optimizing Fuel Retail Operations with SAP Solutions 

Speakers: Jean-Marc Delbos (SAP) & Thomas Weggelaar (Implico) 

Efficient management of fuel retail networks is essential for profitability and customer satisfaction. This joint session with Jean-Marc Delbos from SAP and Thomas Weggelaar from Implico will focus on enhancing the end-to-end processes of fuel retail operations using SAP’s Retail Fuel Network Operations (SAP S/4HANA RFNO) solution and Implico’s automation technologies. 

 Key Takeaways: 

  • Unlocking operational efficiencies through SAP S/4HANA RFNO and automated data flows. 
  • Real-time fuel inventory monitoring and replenishment. 
  • Reducing operational overhead and carbon emissions through process automation. 

 

2. Automatic Trip Planning for Bulk Logistics 

Speaker: Adrian Mägerle (Migrol/Switzerland) & André Bente (Implico) 

(IDM) by Implico is a robust solution designed to enhance capabilities within SAP S4SCSD. Fuel distribution logistics is a complex puzzle that requires precision and efficiency. In this session, Adrian Mägerle from Migrol AG, one of Switzerland’s leading energy services providers, will explore together with André Bente from Implico how automatic trip planning optimizes the logistics in supply chain and secondary distribution by leveraging SAP-integrated tools and Implico’s Integrated Dispatch Management (IDM) capabilities. 

Key Takeaways: 

  • Reducing manual planning efforts  
  • Boosting efficiency and sustainability 
  • Insights from a prototype of the new Implico SAPlement AddTCO 

 

Enhancing Sustainability through Digitalization and the Clean Core Approach 

As an SAP SOLEX Partner, Implico’s approach to sustainability focuses on combining digitalization, automation, and optimized resource allocation with SAP’s Clean Core approach, which ensures that custom business processes are decoupled from the digital core of SAP systems. This strategy allows for easier upgrades, continuous innovation, and long-term system stability. 

By leveraging clean, structured data and the Clean Core methodology, Implico enables: 

  • Efficient transport routes that reduce fuel consumption and emissions. 
  • Real-time energy usage monitoring and sustainability tracking. 
  • Smarter asset utilization to minimize waste and maximize operational efficiency. 

These solutions are designed to meet both current needs and the challenges of a greener future, including the integration of LNG, hydrogen, and other renewable fuels into existing networks. 

 

Join Us in Rotterdam for Insightful Discussions and Live Demos 

We invite attendees to visit our booth for hands-on demos of our solutions and to attend our presentations to learn how we’re transforming supply chains in the energy sector. Whether you’re planning an S/4HANA migration or seeking ways to optimize fuel retail operations, Implico is here to help. 

 

Our Product Portfolio: Driving Efficiency Across the Supply Chain 

Implico’s comprehensive portfolio empowers businesses to streamline processes, reduce costs, and improve customer satisfaction.  

Learn more about our core offerings: 

  • *** REMOVED BY MODERATION *** (Secondary Distribution Management) - Optimizing last-mile fuel delivery through route planning, dispatching, and real-time monitoring. 
  • *** REMOVED BY MODERATION *** (Retail Fuel Network Operations) - Enhancing visibility into fuel station inventory, pricing, and replenishment needs. 
  • *** REMOVED BY MODERATION *** - Automating route planning and dispatch to reduce downtime and maximize resource efficiency. 
  • *** REMOVED BY MODERATION ***- Simplifying and automating processes in bulk liquid handling, from loading to transport. 
  • *** REMOVED BY MODERATION ***- Streamlining cylinder management, automating order processing, and optimizing distribution for maximum efficiency. 

 

Let’s shape the future of energy together! 

Schedule a meeting with our team:  

Visit *** REMOVED BY MODERATION *** to contact us or book a session during the event. 

 

We look forward to seeing you in Rotterdam from April 8-10, 2025!

entry.title: From User Exits to RAP: Adopting a Clean Core Approach in S/4HANA
entry.content:
From User Exits to RAP: Adopting a Clean Core Approach in S/4HANA

Welcome to our SAP blog! In this blog we’re diving into the world of Clean Core in S/4HANA and how you can achieve it using the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP). If you’re looking to future-proof your SAP system while extending its functionalities seamlessly, you’re in the right place. Let’s explore how RAP can help you maintain a cloud-ready, upgrade-safe system without compromising on flexibility.

 

Why Clean Core Matters

In today’s fast-paced digital landscape, businesses need agile and scalable systems. Clean Core is all about keeping your SAP system lean, efficient, and ready for the cloud. It ensures that your customizations don’t interfere with SAP’s standard processes, making upgrades smoother and reducing technical debt.

But here’s the challenge: How do you extend standard functionalities without breaking the core? Traditional methods like User Exits and BAdIs have served us well, but they’re not always cloud-compliant. Enter RAP a modern, event-driven approach to building and extending applications in S/4HANA starting from 2022.

 

Real-World Insights: The Shift to RAP

Through numerous customer engagements, my colleague Balaji @Balaji_Guptha  and I have seen firsthand the challenges of implementing Clean Core. Whether it’s a Brownfield migration or a Greenfield implementation, the goal remains the same: extend without breaking.

While traditional enhancements like User Exits are familiar, they often lead to upgrade headaches. RAP, on the other hand, offers a fresh perspective. It allows you to extend standard processes without modifying the core, ensuring compatibility with future upgrades.

 

The Power of Event-Driven Architecture

At the heart of RAP lies Event-Driven Architecture (EDA). This approach enables systems to react to changes in real-time, making it ideal for modern business processes. With RAP, you can leverage business events to trigger actions, integrate systems, and automate workflows—all while keeping your core clean.

Key Use Cases for RAP Events:

  • Real-Time Notifications: Automatically notify external systems about critical changes, like a new Sales Order or a status update.
  • Seamless System Integration: Enable third-party applications to react to business events without constant polling or synchronous API calls.

RAP Business Event Consumption

SAP provides two approaches to consuming RAP business events:

  1. Remote Event Consumption: Events consumed by external systems (e.g., SAP Event Mesh).
  2. Local Event Consumption: Events handled within the same RAP-based application without external dependencies.

This blog focuses on Local Event Consumption, which enhances system responsiveness and automation within an S/4HANA system.

Local Event Consumption: A Game-Changer

One of the most powerful features of RAP is Local Event Consumption. This allows you to handle events within the same RAP-based application, eliminating the need for external middleware. It’s perfect for scenarios where immediate follow-up actions are required.

How It Works:

  1. Event is Triggered: A change occurs in a RAP business object (e.g., Sales Order creation).
  2. Local Event Handling: An event handler class within the same application processes the event.
  3. Business Logic Execution: The handler class executes predefined logic, such as updating related entities or triggering validations.

Identifying the Standard RAP BO for Business Events

Before implementing local event consumption, identifying the correct RAP Business Object (BO) Root Entity is essential. Follow these steps:

  • Navigate to SAP Business Accelerator Hub https://api.sap.com/

 

  • Select your SAP product (e.g., S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition)

 

  • Locate On-Stack Extensibility → Business Object Interface

 

  • Search for the relevant Business Object, e.g., Sales Order

     

     

  • Once the Business Object (BO) Interface Name is identified, the next step is to navigate to Eclipse ADT (ABAP Development Tools) to determine the Event Binding and Root View Entity Name required for implementing local event consumption in RAP.

  • In Eclipse open the behavior definition for the Business Object interface I_SALESORDERTP.

     

  • In the ABAP repository tree navigate to Business Service Folder to get the Event binding name for the Business Object Interface I_SALESORDERTP.

     

  • Select the required business event, in our case it is Sales Order Created event.

    In the event binding we can find the standard RAP business object with events, R_SALESORDERTP.

     

  • Next navigate to the behavior definition of R_SALESORDERTP business object to check the events enabled for the RAP BO.

     

Identifying Business Events

SAP Provides a standardized way to explore and utilize business events through the SAP Business Accelerator Hub. This platform allows developers and architects to identify events relevant to their business processes and integrate them into an event-driven architecture.

To find the required business event, follow these steps:

  1. Navigate to SAP Business Accelerator Hub 
  2. Select the relevant SAP product (e.g., SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition) based on the system you are working with.
  3. Once the product is selected, navigate to the "Events" tab. This section provides a list of all available events that the selected SAP solution can trigger. Search for the business process or required event in the search bar. In this example we have considered Sales Order Events
  4. Select the event and click on it to view the detailed information.

Use Case: Automating Delivery Block in Sales Orders

Let’s look at a real-world scenario where RAP’s Local Event Consumption shines.

Business Requirement: When a Sales Order is created, the system should automatically apply a delivery block if certain conditions are met. These conditions are based on predefined rules stored in a custom table.

Traditional Approach: In the past, this would be implemented using a User Exit (MV45AFZZ). However, this approach is not cloud-compliant and can lead to upgrade challenges.

Clean Core Approach with RAP: Using RAP’s Local Event Consumption, we can achieve the same functionality without modifying the core. Here’s how:

Creating an Event Handler Class
  1. Define a global class that listens for events from the Sales Order business object.
  2. Use the keyword FOR EVENTS OF to specify the behavior definition.
Inherit from the Event Handler Superclass
  1. In the local class, inherit from CL_ABAP_BEHAVIOR_EVENT_HANDLER to access event-handling capabilities.
Define Event Handling Methods
  1. Create methods to handle specific events, such as SalesOrder.Created.
  2. Category of the custom class created will be ‘Event Handler for Entity Events’.
Implement Business Logic
  1. Logic to enable the delivery block at sales order header level.
  2. In this implementation, the Enablement of the Delivery Block in the sales order is achieved using Entity Manipulation Language (EML) instead of traditional BAPIs. 
  3. Refer to the SAP Help Portal at the following link: https://help.sap.com/docs/abap-cloud/abap-rap/entity-manipulation-language-eml . This resource provides the syntax and detailed explanation for EML.

By implementing this, we successfully automated the application of a delivery block in a cloud-compliant manner, ensuring minimal technical debt and future-proof extensibility. This approach not only aligns with SAP’s Clean Core principles but also enhances system agility and maintainability.

 

Why This Matters

By adopting RAP’s Local Event Consumption, you’re not just solving a business problem—you’re future-proofing your SAP system. This approach ensures that your customizations are cloud-compliant, upgrade-safe, and scalable.

 

Final Thoughts

RAP is more than just a programming model—it’s a mindset shift. It encourages us to rethink how we extend SAP systems, moving away from traditional methods and embracing modern, event-driven architectures. Whether you’re dealing with Sales Orders, Purchase Orders, or any other business process, RAP provides the tools you need to keep your core clean and your business agile. So, what are you waiting for? Start exploring RAP today and unlock the full potential of your S/4HANA system!

Stay tuned for more insights on Clean Core strategies in S/4HANA!

Feel free to ask for any clarifications or share your suggestions!

Regards,

Ravdeep Singh

entry.title: AIF Automatic Reprocessing – Technical deep dive
entry.content:
Application interface framework (AIF) AIF Documentation  is an framework which is widely used in S/4 HANA, Central finance for rationalization of different kind of interfaces with universal monitoring and error handling capabilities, underlying interface technology e.g. IDOC, XML etc remains abstract to the end user (Technical / Business).

With growing use-cases of AIF in large enterprise setting, there comes different challenges and one of them is understanding of automatic reprocessing capabilities.

In this blog, we would deep dive into details of technical know-how of automatic reprocessing.

Use case – We have set up XML/Structured persistence interface in AIF and based on a specific message number, AIF should do the automatic processing in certain interval for certain times.

First Tcode which is used in this process is /n/AIF/REP_AC_DEF where reprocessing action is defined. SAP has delivered standard function module ‘/AIF/RESTART_MSG’ for this purpose which is sufficient in almost all the cases however if you have a custom requirement then custom FM based on template FM ‘/AIF/TEMPL_RESTART_AUTO_REPR’ can be created and assigned in this Tcode.

Assigned FM would be used during reprocessing job, it will pick the properties/action/logic from original interface configuration for which GUID is being reprocessed.

Assigned runtime config Id(Tcode - /n/AIF/PERS_CGR) determines how the reprocessing should occur, e.g. packet size, server group etc.

The automatic reprocessing is triggered by specific error messages for a registered AIF interface.

So the user has to register the AIF Interface and the corresponding error messages by message class and number for automatic reprocessing in the transaction /n/AIF/REP_AC_ASGN.

 

  1. Assign the reprocessing action which was defined in previous transaction.
  2. Max repr. Counter - Define a maximum number of reprocessing attempts that can be executed for a failed data message.
  3. Minimum wait time (Seconds / Minutes /  Hours) - Define a minimum waiting time that must pass before the application can trigger a reprocessing attempt. 
    e.g. If a vendor is blocked and it takes around 6 hours for master data team to unblock the vendor then Minimum waiting time can be defined as 6 hours so that reprocessing can be attempted after vendor is unblocked.
  4.  Maximum wait time (Seconds / Minutes /  Hours) - Define a maximum waiting time before which             application must trigger a reprocessing attempt.
    e.g. if you are supposed to provide a acknowledgment of original AIF message with in 10 hours then maximum waiting time could be 10 hours so that all reprocessing attempts can be made to get the latest status based on the error.
  5. Intermediate status (In Process or Error) the failed data message displays while it's waiting to be reprocessed.

Conclusion –

  1. First Reprocessing iteration should start only after “Min time” + original AIF message creation time has elapsed but No later than “Max time” + original AIF message creation time
  2. Next retry attempt of reprocessing job would occur after previous iteration completion time + “Max time” , if same errors still exists

Below FM / class method are called during original AIF message processing if the AIF error message meets the reprocessing criteria and it schedules the job based on defined reprocessing config.

 

 

Once above is executed, a job is created in ‘Scheduled’ status and can be checked in SM37. Planned start date for these jobs are defined based on Min and Max time defined in AIF reprocessing config.

Job name - /AIF/Namespace_RuntimeConfigID*

Reprocessing data is captured in table /AIF/REP_MSG_CNT which provides valuable information like how many retry attempts are made , timestamp etc.

Working example – Lets check how this config works with a working example-

We have set the config as below where maximum retries are 2, Minimum waiting time is 1 Minute and Maximum waiting time is 10 Mis.

Chain of events-

  1. Original AIF message was created on 15:38 which would schedule reprocessing job.
  2. First reprocessing job was released with a scheduled start time of 15:48 , the original message was created on 15:38 however ‘Max time’ was defined as 10 mins so reprocessing jobs has been scheduled for original + 10 mins.
  3. Second reprocessing was released with a scheduled start time of 15:58 since first iteration was completed on 15:48 so first iteration + 10 Mins.

AIF Message job log with timings.

While AIF message is waiting to be reprocessed, the status becomes ‘In Progress’ and sub status would be ‘Automatic reprocessing’ which can be checked in corresponding index table.

 

Tips –

  1. Reprocessing Configuration performed for defining actions and assigning message is not transportable and should be done in each client separately.
  2. If there is an existing job scheduled between the minimum waiting time and the maximum waiting time, the reprocessing message is assigned to this job insteda of creating a new job.
  3. Runtime Configuration Group should be active and use Run Scheduled.

entry.title: SAP just launched the Business Suite - what does this mean for Finance?
entry.content:
Feb 13, 2025 is “Business Unleashed Day” for the SAP Community, with announcements on SAP Business Suite and SAP Business Data Cloud - and how AI, data, and applications are combined like never before to help you unleash your full potential and make you unstoppable.

Whether your company has been running an SAP ERP solution for many years, or whether you’re just at the beginning of the evaluation of a Cloud ERP solution, you might be wondering what impact these announcements – especially the announcements of the SAP Business Suite - will have on your landscape planning.

Evolution from monolithic to composable

Just as technology has completely changed since ERP was first defined for 1970s mainframes, SAP’s approach to ERP has evolved to keep up you keep us with the needs of your business:

  • Modular capabilities that can be adopted quickly as new needs arise
  • Always up-to-date software-as-a-service solutions that give you non-disruptive access to innovations
  • Clear interoperability and data semantics so that you will be in the best position to realize the benefits of AI

The 2025 Gartner® ERP Primer for 2025 states that “in Gartner’s 2023 ERP, Procurement, HCM and Finance Apps Survey of those manager level and above who are responsible for ERP decision making, 71% reported their organizations were either in process or have plans to adopt a flexible and agile ERP architecture within two years.”

So, chances are, you’re already thinking about the importance of having the flexibility to choose business technology more flexibly to support your growth initiatives.

 

 

Gartner: ERP Primer for 2025, by Denis Torii, Dixie John. GARTNER is a registered trademark and service mark of Gartner, Inc. and/or its affiliates in the U.S. and internationally and is used herein with permission. All rights reserved.

 

The SAP Business Suite is SAP’s answer to Composable ERP

You know SAP as a leader in Cloud ERP for years, but the “ERP and Finance” focus of finance teams may overlook the additional, incremental value SAP can provide with our broader family of solutions – solutions that address business needs and can be adopted incrementally, like Fieldglass, Ariba, SuccessFactors, Concur, and CX.

Since the interoperability and data semantics among the applications is curated by SAP, the insights that can be achieved with SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP Business AI are limitless.

 

RISE with SAP is a transformation journey aimed at helping SAP ERP customers transition to the SAP Business Suite - so they can stay ahead of the competition by continuously innovating – across their whole enterprise landscape – not just in “ERP”.

What this means is that Finance teams should be thinking of the value that SAP solutions can create in your IT landscape. For example, adopting updated, world-class procurement processes have benefits specifically for the CFO in terms of cash visibility, risk mitigation and more. You can read a great blog on the topic here.

The SAP Business Suite approach encourages companies to think beyond their administrative ERP footprint: how can we adopt the best solutions to solve all types of business problems – for the Chief Revenue Officer, or for the Chief Human Resources Officer?

Cloud software and software-as-a-service promised that business teams would be able to pick-and-choose the services that meet their business requirements. However, often IT does have to be involved to manage integrations and ensure data interoperability. The SAP Business Suite aims to make that modularity - and business user choice - promise a reality.  

And modularity is not just part of the North Star architecture at the line of business level. SAP Business Suite is a reminder that within the line of business solutions, there are software-as-a-service solutions that can be adopted to solve specific business problems – even if your current core architecture might be more monolithic than you’d like.

Composability within Financial Management

Even within the areas that are traditionally considered to be “ERP”, SAP is offering more flexibility and modularity than ever before. Based on your business needs, these SaaS solutions – with their out-of-the-box best practices and constant access to innovation - are available as extensions to SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud:

  • SAP Green Ledger - to align your carbon and financial accounting by integrating carbon emissions with your business processes, complementing SAP’s portfolio of sustainability solutions, including SAP Sustainability Control Tower.
  • SAP Advanced Financial Closing – for orchestrating financial closing activities across ERP systems
  • SAP Account Substantiation and Automation by BlackLine – to automate manual closing tasks and reconciliations
  • SAP Digital Banking – to automate connections to any financial service provider (banks, SWIFT, payment service providers) in a quick and easy way for more control
  • SAP Cloud for Credit Integration – to integrate and manage external credit rating information for completely touchless, responsive credit decisionmaking
  • SAP Trading Platform Integration – to connect to leading trading platform providers for inbound and outbound straight-through-processing of trades 
  • SAP Risk and Assurance Management - to automate compliance checks and manage remediations efficiently

These solutions – that solve specific business problems- are integrated with our standard financial management processes in Cloud ERP.

So, if you’re part of the 70% of ERP leaders thinking about flexibility in ERP, you should consider what the SAP Business Suite can do for your organization. Making the most of the SAP Business Suite will help you

  • Manage uncertainty through effective steering and agile planning
  • Navigate transformation supported by efficient processes to balance growth and profitability
  • Enable compliance and sustainability in an ever-evolving regulatory and risk environment

Learn more about Finance solutions here.

entry.title: Production Planning Master Data: Bill of Material
entry.content:
What is Bill of Material in Production Planning?

In a simple word, Bill of Material (BOM) is a structured list of all components (raw materials, semi-finished goods, finished goods) required to manufacture a finished product. It defines the relationship between a product and its components in Production Planning and Material Requirements Planning (MRP). BOM are use in several manufacturing types such as discrete manufacturing, process manufacturing, and repetitive manufacturing in SAP system.


Example of Bill of Material table


Example of Bill of Material structure

Bill of material is used to determine the required quantity of components (MRP), defines the production process and operations for the materials in the BOM, production order who used BOM to consume components in manufacturing, and also used to calculate the material costing. SAP will calculate the price for each material in the bill of material to be included in the cost of goods manufactured.

SAP supports various BOM types depending on the production and business requirements:

  • Material BOM – Used in production to define product structures.
  • Equipment BOM – Used in Plant Maintenance (PM) for maintenance planning.
  • Functional Location BOM – Used for maintenance at a specific location.
  • Sales Order BOM – Specific to customer requirements in Make-to-Order (MTO) scenarios.
  • Variant BOM – Supports configurable materials with different variations.
  • Multi-Level BOM – A hierarchical BOM where components also have their own sub-components.


Now let’s talk about how to create a BOM.

You can go to CS01 and insert the material, plant, and BOM usage. 
Then, go to the BOM header.

Choose the BOM status ‘1’ if you want to set the BOM active.

Then insert the components for this material. Also choose the item category and quantity.

So that's basically how you create a BOM. Feel free to discuss more if you have any other insights or questions!

A lifetime learner,
Dafina
